political.s: FEC data confirms CVS Health's PAC leans right, having raised $1.1M with Republican recipients receiving the majority share. Per OpenSecrets, CVS gave ~$2.95M in total contributions in the 2024 cycle and spent $9.46M on federal lobbying in 2024, focusing on healthcare and pharmacy policy. PAC recipient data shows roughly 64% to Republicans and 36% to Democrats at the party level, consistent with a right-leaning posture.

charity.s: The CVS Health Foundation provided over $21.4M in grants in 2024 across 753 awards, focused on healthcare access, chronic disease management, food insecurity, and tobacco cessation. Strategic programs like Health Zones and Project Health deliver free community screenings and multi-million-dollar investments in underserved areas nationwide. The company also runs employee volunteer grants and scholarship programs for colleagues' children.
charity.focus: Healthcare access, chronic disease, food insecurity, tobacco prevention, workforce development

environment.s: CVS Health has committed to net-zero GHG emissions across its value chain by 2050 and targets a 47% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 2030 from a 2019 baseline. The company was previously named to CDP's A-List for climate change action and participates in the Science Based Targets initiative. Total reported GHG emissions in 2025 were approximately 28.6 billion kg CO2e, with Scope 3 (supply chain) dominating at ~95% of the footprint.

labor.s: CVS faced a significant unfair labor practice strike in 2024 when UFCW Local 324 workers at seven Southern California stores walked out over alleged unlawful worker surveillance, retaliation for union activity, and blocked union access. Workers also reported that average store clerks earn under $20/hour and that 64% of surveyed union members lacked CVS health coverage due to high costs. A tentative agreement was eventually reached, but the episode reflects ongoing labor tensions at the retail level.

dei.s: CVS Health has historically earned a perfect 100 score on the HRC Corporate Equality Index, including in 2025, and has robust DEI infrastructure including a VP of DEI&B, the INCLUDE training program rolled out to all ~300,000 employees, and resource groups for LGBTQ+ colleagues. However, the company quietly deleted its 'Supplier Diversity' webpage in February 2025, signaling potential rollback under the broader corporate DEI retreat. CVS ties senior manager compensation to diversity and inclusion metrics and publishes annual EEO-1 reports.

animals.s: CVS affirmed a 'no animal testing' policy for its store brand products in 2008 and works with suppliers to enforce this standard throughout product development. The company also achieved its goal of sourcing 100% cage-free eggs by end of 2022 and has collaborated with PETA on public service campaigns. However, CVS retail stores carry third-party brands that do test on animals, and as a pharmacy CVS dispenses FDA-regulated drugs that are tested on animals by law, making a fully cruelty-free designation inapplicable at the enterprise level.

guns.s: CVS Health does not sell firearms or ammunition in any of its retail pharmacy locations. The company's product assortment is focused on health, wellness, and personal care items. No gun-related controversy or policy issue has been associated with CVS Health.

privacy.s: CVS has a troubled privacy track record: it paid $2.25M in a landmark HIPAA settlement after employees improperly disposed of protected health information, and in 2025 a federal judge allowed a class action to proceed alleging CVS used third-party tracking pixels to collect and share users' sensitive health data with advertisers without consent. Separate suits allege session replay software captured users' keystrokes and prescription-related activity on the CVS website. These recurring issues place CVS's data privacy practices below industry expectations.

execPay.s: CVS Health CEO David Joyner received $17.8–$18.2M in total compensation in 2024, yielding a CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio of 299:1, with the median employee earning approximately $60,917 annually. This ratio, while lower than some health insurance peers (e.g., Elevance at 370:1), remains extremely high relative to broader industry norms. Executive pay packages are primarily equity-driven and have faced shareholder scrutiny in past proxy votes.

primaryOffenses.category: off-label or unapproved promotion of medical products
off-label or unapproved promotion of medical products
